data.title: Marvell Technology: Don't Get Tempted By The 42% Dip
data.description:
Marvell Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ:MRVL) is down 42% YTD, and the company’s 1QFY26 report, which came out at the end of May, didn’t help reverse this downward spiral; in fact, losses deepened post-earnings. We don’t see any near-term catalyst to turn around Marvell’s fortunes in the near-term, so we reiterate Sell on the stock.

What investors need to understand about Marvell is that its stock rallied late last year, going from $56 per share in August to about $124 in December. The stock was on a streak due to its custom AI ASIC, but for the last two earnings calls it’s disappointed, which is easy to mistake for a case of the weakness being priced in, but we think Marvell will continue to struggle in the near term after last year’s rally put a lot of eyes on the stock as an underrated AI play. While that was true then, it is no longer applicable today, and there are three reasons why.

Reason #1: Expectations are (still) much too high

Marvell jumped onto the AI bandwagon on demand for its electro-optical position and ramp of its custom ASIC; these two get factored into the company’s data center-related sales, which have been growing sequentially from $816.4M in 1QFY25 to $1.44B in 1QFY26.

The good news is that Marvell is benefiting from increased port count and expansion of the electro-optical market; the bad news is that this positive already got priced into the stock and Street expectations for Marvell’s AI revenue growth, which now sit at 40% for the current year, higher than what we think is realistic. We’ve been flagging this high expectation for a while, but after we got insight into the first two quarters of Marvell’s FY26, we are confident that this number is much too high. For the quarter reported in May, Marvell told us that its data center-related revenues grew 5.5% sequentially, marking the slowest sequential growth since 1QFY24, which is now two years ago, and management is expecting data center-related revenues to only grow another 5% next quarter. This would mean that 2HFY26 would have to have hyper growth for the company to meet, let alone outperform, Wall Street’s 40% expectation.

The chart below outlines Marvell’s quarterly revenue by business line, and this chart is meant to show you two things. The first is that the pace of growth in the company’s data center-related sales has moderated for the past two quarters, which has been consistent with the stock selling off after each of those quarters. We expect this slower pace to continue as Marvell’s largest ASIC customer, Amazon (AMZN), has already been priced into outlook.

The company has two other ASIC customers, but guess what? No one does single source anymore, meaning none of the hyperscalers put all their eggs in one basket, so Marvell’s ASIC customers, for the most part, are also Broadcom (AVGO) customers, and Broadcom, unlike Marvell, has scale on its side.

The second thing this chart should show is the pace of growth in enterprise networking and carrier infrastructure sales; these two markets entered correction during 1HFY25 and have only recently begun to recover. Now, in spite of their recovery over the past two quarters, the stock has sold off, which means that the market is more interested in Marvell’s data center sales than anything else. This means that if the data center pace of growth doesn’t pick up, then Marvell will be looking at an even sharper disappointment come 2HFY26.

Reason #2: Gross margins are getting slim for the summer

Now, let’s say Marvell grows its ASIC business, which sounds like it should be the best case scenario for the company, what would happen? The answer is pretty simple: margins would take a hit. The problem for Marvell is that they do not have the benefit of scale or economies of scale, for that matter, meaning when they scale their ASIC business, their margins contract. Marvell’s margins have already been contracting for the past five quarters consecutively, as shown below.

The reason for the contraction is the ASIC business. Management reported non-GAAP gross margin of 59.8%, contracting 30 basis points due to ASIC representing a higher percent of the sales mix. Heading into Q2, management is guiding for gross margins to remain in the 59% to 60% range. The problem is that gross margin contraction is an inescapable part of ASIC business; management got asked about this on their earnings call in May by Timothy Arcuri from UBS; Tim asked about AI as part of the data center mix and if lower gross margins were the result of custom silicon growing faster during the quarter. Management, Matt Murphy, to be specific, handled the question, answering that:

...clearly the custom business we have does run at a fundamentally lower gross margin. So that is going to modulate our gross margins on a quarterly basis and you've seen the impact, but we've also had, I think, strong recovery in some of the other businesses as well. But that's always been fundamentally the custom business a lower than company average gross margin.

Unlike Broadcom, Marvell’s other end markets cannot offset this. Enterprise networking and carrier infrastructure are recovering, but their strength is not sufficient to support margin expansion. This is especially when we remember management told us that “Data center was our largest end market contributing 76% of total revenue” in this earnings call. Of that data center revenue, data center AI made up most of the mix, speculatively at +55% of the mix this quarter.

So, all in all, if Marvell is to meet Street expectations for AI revenue growth, it needs to expand ASIC business, and if it does that, margins will take a hit. Don't take it from us, take it from management. In another section of the Q&A, Murphy added that:

...within custom, there is a range, and it's like the old adage in semiconductors. The higher the volume, you're going to see lower gross margins.

Reason #3: Pricey, pricey, pricey

Marvell is expensive; the stock trades above the peer group average on almost every metric. On forward EV/Sales, Marvell trades at 6.97x compared to a group average of 2.82x, and on forward P/E, 60.18x versus a group average of 28.38x. The chart below shows the full scoop on Marvell’s multiples.

The stock is up over the past month by 7%, but this is part of the broader market recovery after the Trump tariff panic rather than a specific sign of momentum for Marvell, in our opinion. If you look at the company’s closest competitor, Broadcom, the stock is one of the best-performing semi stocks, up 57% since the start of the quarter, versus Marvell, up 8% during the same period. To top it off, Marvell’s RSI is also closer to overbought territory than oversold, currently at 58, which makes the case to buy on weakness after the YTD pullback all the more unattractive in our opinion.

Bottom Line: Great company, wrong time

Marvell is a great company; we're fans. That said, we don't see an attractive risk-reward for the stock this year. The risk for more pressure on its financials outweighs its position within the AI market at the moment. We think it'll be increasingly hard for them to impress investors unless they get an additional ASIC customer, and even then, that'll come at the expense of their margins. Every way we look at it, Marvell could see more downside ahead, even though a lot of the weakness has been priced in after the YTD drop.

data.title: Marimekko Starts FY25 On Good Footing But Is More Expensive Than LVMH
data.description:
Marimekko’s (OTCPK:MKKOF) 1Q25 results show a relatively resilient operation in most geographies, with a positive outlook despite seasonal or non-recurrent factors affecting margins, particularly in the high-margin licensing business.

Sales continued to grow in key geographies and management reaffirmed guidance for the year, both in revenue and margins. This is a positive signal in a weak global apparel environment.

Unfortunately, the stock has barely moved since my last article and continues to trade at a level I consider unreasonably high for an apparel brand. I maintain my Hold.

1Q25 results

Global growth: Marimekko posted sales growth across all its key geographies.

In Finland, its largest market, the company reported an increase despite tough comps due to a more promotional wholesale environment last year. Retail sales in Finland were up 9%.

In Asia-Pacific, Marimekko’s most important market at the brand level (though not in consolidated financials due to partner-operated stores), growth continued ex-licensing. Retail sales were up 16% (online stores operated by Marimekko) and wholesale was up 4% (partner stores). Brand sales, which include partner retail sales and licensing product sales, were down 6%. The company's APAC revenue declined 2%, however, due to a large drop in licensing income.

Other markets, including Scandinavia, continental Europe, and North America, developed positively as well. While they represent smaller absolute contributions (25% of revenues in aggregate), they still show that the company is performing in several markets.

Licensing hit: The main drag on 1Q25 performance was the sharp drop in licensing income (-70% aggregate and -100% in APAC, as shown on the earnings report).

This had been flagged in the 4Q24 report and again in the 1Q25 release as a development that will impact all of FY25. Licensing income is volatile due to its lumpy nature, such as large initial payments or specific collaborative drops, which in FY23/24 included big names such as Ikea or Adidas.

The company says the impact will be 'significant' to licensing income in FY25. In FY24, licensing income reached EUR 3.5 million, which is almost entirely operating profit given the nearly 100% gross margin nature of the category. If the impact is 70%, like in 1Q25 (probably lower because 1Q24 had already booked 40% of licensing profit from the year), then the impact to operating profits will be EUR 2 million (or 6% of operating profit and 1pp of margin).

Costs/investment keep going up: Despite weak licensing and an uncertain macro, the company is maintaining or even increasing its investments, which can also be called costs.

Marketing spend, which reached EUR 10 million in FY24, is expected to grow further in FY25. In 1Q, Marimekko participated in the Copenhagen Fashion Week, opened three pop-up stores in Asia, three new permanent stores in Europe, and another in Tokyo (partner-operated), and also launched an itinerant exhibition in Asia. Marketing is in part used for all of these activities, but also to sustain sales via 'low-calorie' digital sales (i.e., digital sales that bump revenues but generate little profits because the CAC is high).

Headcount and SG&A also continued to increase. With sales largely flat and licensing down, this led to a 200bps decline in operating margins. The company frames this as a long-term brand-building strategy. While I understand the rationale, I remain skeptical of such moves in the context of a consumer discretionary environment that remains highly uncertain.

Valuation above the best of the industry, not attractive

Management maintained FY25 guidance, which expects sales to be flat to modestly up versus EUR 182 million in FY24. Surprisingly, operating margins are expected to be flat to slightly up (16–19% range, compared to 17.5% in FY24). Given the 1pp headwind from licensing alone and the expanding SG&A base, this seems optimistic. Unfortunately, the earnings call did not include questions addressing how this would be achieved, so I remain unconvinced.

Even taking the company’s guidance at face value, we are looking at EUR 30–35 million in operating income and EUR 22–25 million in net income. The company paid out most of its net income as dividends in FY24 (EUR 15 million), so most of the income becomes actual returns for shareholders.

Against this, Marimekko trades at a market cap of EUR 550 million, or 22 to 25 times earnings. This is very expensive for an apparel name operating at or near cyclical peak margins. Compare it to LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF), probably the best higher-end brand operator in the world, trading at around 20x.

A high multiple is bad for two reasons. First, it leaves very limited room for upside from here unless global conditions improve significantly, and second, it opens a compound risk of worse economic conditions, worsening sales and margins (i.e. lower profits) coupled with multiple compression.

As I mentioned in my first article on the brand, I believe Marimekko is a quality brand with unique assets, a strong aesthetic identity, and a history of competent management. But it is not LVMH, and yet it trades at a higher multiple. I maintain my Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lands' End: At This Valuation, Hold Your Nose And Buy
data.description:
We have suggested several trades in Lands' End (NASDAQ:LE) over the last few years. Nearly all of them have worked out except for our last call for a swing trade which came just before the market sold off, and tariffs rocked retailers hard. Shares are down almost in half and while stop losses would have limited the damage, it was still a sight to behold. However, at this valuation, and all impacts of tariffs seemingly baked in, the stock is at a hold your nose and buy level. Many specialty retailers are in a similar boat. They face huge competition and the tariffs are a costly impact. But the valuation has eroded so much, especially in the last few months, shares are at a level where the risk-reward is incredibly favorable. These are the setups we model and look for, with extended research, entry points, and exits, at BAD BEAT Investing. LE stock is a decent candidate for a swing back to double-digit share prices, and would be double-digit trading returns from this level. Let us discuss the just-reported earnings, which have us bullish.

A specialty retailer, with a number of brands

This is a specialty retailer folks, with a number of different brands. Like competitors it offers its customers casual clothing, swimwear, shoes, and home products. There is of course the flagship banner Lands' End, but also brands like Lands' End Lighthouse, Drifter, and several others. Specialty retail is tough and for months, Lands' End, Inc. stock, as well as many competitors, have been crushed. But is it all that bad?

Q1 results in context

Looking at Q1 revenue, sales were still down 8.5% from last year hitting $261.2 million missing consensus by $11.4 million. As a reminder, this is really a digitally focused retailer and global online revenue was $227.7 million, a decrease of $1 million from $228.7 million a year ago. U.S. online revenue was $170.7 million, an increase of 0.2% from $170.5 million a year ago. There was ongoing strength in their outerwear products, but a slow start to the swimwear season. The “Outfitters” brand saw sales of $42.9 million, rising 0.5% from $42.7 million last year. This quarter, school uniform sales were a touch light, but business uniforms performed well. What about margin power?

Margins and earnings power

The company has made strong efforts to boost its margins. This has continued. Here in Q1, the company saw gross margin expansion of approximately 210 basis points, hitting 50.8% rising from 48.7% a year ago. But due to lower sales, total gross profit was lower. However, the margin expansion is impressive. Adjusted EBITDA was $9.5 million, down from $11.6 million a year ago. This was a result of lower sales primarily, while selling expenses and operational expenses were generally well-controlled. In terms of inventory, we saw strong inventory turnover. The company saw a 9% reduction in year-over-year inventory, suggesting that the company is efficiently moving products. The efficiency efforts and sped to market initiatives put into place have been paying off. This continues a run of significant inventory turnover and margin improvement. This is usually a money losing quarter, but less money was lost than last year which is positive. Adjusted net loss was $5.4 million of $0.18 per share. This is better than the losses of $6.2 million or $0.20 per share last year.

We also want to point out that despite the company losing money on an EPS basis, and cash balances coming down, the company still repurchased $2.8 million in shares. The balance sheet is in relatively good shape. There is $243 million in term loan debt outstanding, a $13 million improvement from a year ago, and $40 million of borrowings on its ABL facility (the same as a year ago).

Forward view, and a strategic plan

We like buying here again for a trade for two reasons. One, operationally and despite tariff risk, the company is in better shape than a year ago. And second we do know that the company is in the middle of a strategic alternative process which could include a sale or a merger. But without it, the outlook is good. A word on tariffs here. The company is assuming a baseline tariff of approximately 10% in all countries except China, which accounted for less than 8% of the company's product cost in 2024 and where we assume a 30% tariff. We surmise it is moving to be even less dependent on China moving forward. As we look ahead, for fiscal 2025, sales look to be $1.33 to $1.45 billion. That means the sales declines are likely done if the range hits toward the mid- to higher-end. That is positive. Adjusted EPS is guided at a wide $0.48 to $0.86. Folks, if it hits the midpoint of $0.67, the stock is trading at 12X FWD. That is a solid valuation. It also means growth of 65% from last year. We think you buy here at $8.

data.title: Vigil Neuroscience: ALSP Data Doesn't Deliver, But Sanofi Purchase Remains Intact
data.description:
Recently, Vigil Neuroscience, Inc. (NASDAQ:VIGL) announced an update relating to its development of fully human monoclonal antibody TREM2 agonist iluzanebart for the treatment of patients with adult-onset leukoencephalopathy with axonal spheroids and pigmented glia [ALSP]. It was noted that this drug passed with flying colors when it came to safety and tolerability profile across both doses tested [20 mg/kg and 40 mg/kg] of it, but that it failed to show any beneficial effect when it came to possible biomarker improvement or other efficacy endpoints in the targeting of these patients.

With this failed phase 2 open-label IGNITE trial not delivering for the company, it is expected to return rights of iluzanebart back to Amgen (AMGN). The stock remains a "Hold" rating because despite this mid-stage trial failing, and Sanofi (SNY) is still expected to acquire Vigil Neurosciences for approximately $470 million. The whole point of the acquisition of this small-cap biotech actually had nothing to do with the development of iluzanebart at all. Instead, the whole premise was for Sanofi to get its hands on Vigil's oral small molecule TREM2 agonist VG-3927 for the treatment of patients with Alzheimer's disease [AD].

This makes sense because, as I will show below, there was the release of positive phase 1 single-ascending dose [SAD] and multiple-ascending dose [MAD] study data released using this specific TREM2 agonist to treat these AD patients. With the continued acquisition of Vigil Neuroscience By Sanofi still on track for Q3 of 2025, plus the potential for shareholders to receive a Contingent Value Right [CVR] of $2.00 per share upon first commercialization of VG-3927 for the treatment of patients with AD, I believe for these reasons that this stock remains a "Hold" rating.

Phase 2 Open-Label IGNITE Study In ALSP Doesn't Deliver Any Value For Shareholders

As I noted directly above, Vigil Neuroscience announced an update relating to its phase 2 open-label IGNITE study, which used fully human monoclonal antibody TREM2 agonist for the treatment of patients with adult-onset leukoencephalopathy with axonal spheroids and pigmented glia. Before going over the mid-stage trial itself that was released by this company, it is important to mention what this disorder is and why it is highly devastating for these patients.

Adult-onset leukoencephalopathy with axonal spheroids and pigmented glia [ALSP] is characterized as a leukoencephalopathy in which white matter of the brain is altered. This white matter consists of nerve fibers [axons] which are protected by myelin and transmit nerve impulses throughout the body. The alteration off white matter is shown as a lesion on the brain in an MRI, which is how it is known. The bottom line is that damage to myelin and neurons of a patient are common hallmarks of ALSP disease. Symptoms of this disorder vary a lot and can range from depression/memory loss all the way to loss of functional thinking and motor skills/muscle loss.

The phase 2 Open-label IGNITE study failed to deliver in terms of iluzanebart being able to help treat these patients. The company didn't share much in terms of clinical efficacy data at all. All that was noted was that this fully human monoclonal antibody TREM2 agonist didn't have an effect on improving biomarkers for these patients. Nor, was it shown that any clinical efficacy endpoints saw any type of major improvement. A positive would be at least that this drug given at both 20 mg/kg and 40 mg/kg doses was safe/tolerable for patients to take.

It is a shame how this program ended up, and the reason why I state that is because there was a lot of potential in place when the company first reported positive interim data from this IGNITE study back on November 16th of 2023. At that time, it noted that iluzanebart was able to show positive changes in terms of neurofilament light chain [NfL] and MRI biomarkers in terms of ventricular volume and gray matter volume as well.

The disorder of ALSP occurs due to a mutation of the CSF1R gene, which results in microglial dysfunction. Both TREM2 and CSF1R transduce biological effects of allowing for cell survival and proliferation. This effect is replicated through signaling partner DAP12/SYK. The goal of iluzanebart was to transduce signaling through this partner signaling agent and compensate for CSF1R loss observed in ALSP patients. The end result? To restore microglial function and reduce disease burden for these patients.

Unfortunately, this was not achieved as intended. With that being said, the company noted in its release of data, that it intends to discontinue the phase 2 long-term open-label study. As I noted before, the final outcome is that the company will return rights of iluzanebart back to Amgen before the closing of the acquisition of Vigil in Q3 of 2025.

Sanofi Acquisition Of Vigil Neuroscience Presses On Without Issue

The truth is that while it is disappointing that Vigil Neuroscience will not be able to continue developing iluzanebart for the treatment of patients with ALSP, it is not going to matter much if at all. Despite this phase 2 open-label IGNITE study failing, Sanofi is still in place to acquire Vigil. Again, it is solely doing so because of the ongoing development of oral small molecule TREM2 agonist VG-3927.

With that being said, the deal that was laid out was for this big pharmaceutical company to acquire Vigil Neuroscience for approximately $470 million. In other words, the deal itself entails shareholders of the small-cap company to receive up to $10.00 per share in cash. This is to split up as $8.00 per share in cash at closing of this transaction and then the $2.00 per share CVR to potentially be obtained upon the first commercial sale of VG-3927 for the treatment of patients with Alzheimer's disease [AD]. The global Alzheimer's therapeutics market size is expected to reach $15.19 billion by 2030. If everything goes accordingly, then Sanofi should complete its acquisition of Vigil Neuroscience by Q3 of 2025.

It makes perfect sense why Sanofi would want to acquire Vigil Neuroscience for its oral small molecule TREM2 agonist VG-3927. Why would this be the case? There are actually two reasons why this is the case. The first reason being that this is a phase 2-ready clinical asset. In essence, it is ready to be evaluated in a mid-stage study targeting such a neurological disorder patient population.

Secondly, it is because, as I will be going over now, Vigil reported positive results from its phase 1 SAD and MAD study using VG-3927 to treat AD patients. The pharmacokinetic [PK] profile was ideal and lends the ability to have convenient once-daily oral dosing for these patients. More importantly, it was noted that soluble TREM2 [sTREM2] was reduced in a dose-dependent 50% manner in Cerebral Spinal Fluid [CSF]. Why is this an important finding? That's because sTREM2 activity on microglia [found on surface of microglial cells] is increased and leads to neuroinflammation. Thus, the goal is to reduce sTREM2 as much as possible.

The end goal for this particular drug is TREM2 agonism [increase in microglial function] to clear inflammation and pathologic aggregates in the patient's brain. The hope is that this will prove to be the case upon Sanofi taking over phase 2 testing of this oral small molecule TREM2 agonist drug.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Vigil Neuroscience had cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities of $87.1 million as of March 31st of 2025. The truth is that it believed that this cash on hand would only be enough for a cash runway to fund its operations into 2026. The company was burning through approximately $23.5 million in cash per quarter. This was broken down into $16.5 million in R&D expenses and $7.0 million in G&A expenses. Had this biotech needed to continue its operations on its own, then it would definitely need to raise additional cash.

However, it is in a unique situation in that it is expected to be acquired by Sanofi in Q3 of 2025. With that being said, if the acquisition of Vigil by Sanofi closes at that expected time, then there would be no need to worry about the small-caps financial backing. Sanofi would be able to handle the expense of running a phase 2 study of VG-3927 for the treatment of patients with AD on its own.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of with Vigil Neuroscience. The first risk is the acquisition of it by Sanofi. This transaction has not yet been completed, as it is not expected to close until Q3 of 2025. The risk here is that there is no certainty that this acquisition will occur. If Vigil ends up not being acquired, then it would be forced to advance VG-3927 on its own. Plus, it would be on the hook financially to do so.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the expected phase 2 study of VG-3927 for the treatment of patients with Alzheimer's disease. Such a mid-stage trial was expected to start in Q3 of 2025 and upon eventual completion of it, there can be no assurance that this mid-stage trial is going to end up achieving statistical significance in its intended primary endpoint. Nor, that the data is going to be adequate enough for it to be able to get a green light from the FDA to move towards phase 3 clinical testing of this oral small molecule TREM2 agonist.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the competition that Sanofi might end up facing when it comes to the development of VG-3927 for the treatment of patients with AD. There is Novartis (NVS), which is expected to initiate a phase 2 study using its fully human monoclonal antibody that selectively activates TREM2 to clear pathogens and other debris in AD patients. Such a mid-stage trial is expected to start in the 2nd half of 2025 and this could end up being a competing drug. Especially, since the mechanism of action [MOA] of this drug is to also establish microglial activation to reduce disease burden.

Conclusion

Vigil Neuroscience remains a Hold rating, and it is highly likely that it will be acquired by Sanofi in Q3 of 2025. Again, the main reason for the big pharma is doing so is in order to get its hands on the oral small molecule TREM2 agonist VG-3927 for the treatment of patients with AD. Despite the failure of the phase 2 open-label IGNITE study, in using iluzanebart for the treatment of patients with ALSP, the big pharma is still expected to acquire this small-cap pharmaceutical company. Sanofi intends to initiate a phase 2 study using VG-3927 to target patients with Alzheimer's Disease. I noted the risk about Novartis above with its TREM2 agonist VHB937 drug candidate, in terms of also being developed to target this very same neurodegenerative patient population. Well, the thing is that Vigil believes its oral small molecule TREM2 agonist might have advantages.

First and foremost, it is not a fully human antibody like other TREM2 agonists that have failed in the past. It is also stated to have developed this drug to not bind to soluble sTREM2. It is believed that this could reduce the risk of Amyloid-related Imaging abnormalities [ARIA] seen with anti-amyloid drugs being given to patients. The lack of sTREM2 binding is expected to counter this. No Fc domain region, compared to monoclonal antibodies [mAbs], could mean lack of ARIA. Lastly, small molecule inhibitors have a shorter half-life, which even if ARIA is observed, dosing could be adjusted to actually account for this. With the continuation of the acquisition of this company by Sanofi, plus the expected start of a phase 2 study using VG-3927 for the treatment of patients AD [both in Q3 of 2025], I believe that this stock remains a Hold rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dollar Tree Stores: Valuation Is Still Cheap Given The Growth Outlook
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Dollar Tree Stores (NASDAQ:DLTR) was a buy rating because the business was not only benefiting from the economic downturn but also had a cleaner business model after the liquidation of Family Dollar. The stock is up ~26% since my last write-up, but I think there’s more room to run. With the MPP strategy clearly working, macro tailwinds still intact, and margin expanding, I remain bullish. Valuation remains undemanding, and I believe the setup continues to favor multiple expansion as fundamentals strengthen.

1Q25 results update

1Q25 Dollar Tree net sales grew 11.3% y/y to $4.637 billion, driven by same-store sales growth [SSSG] of 1.7%, and notably it was entirely driven by a 2.1% increase in traffic, which suggests that the brand continues to be relevant to cost-conscious consumers. The average ticket fell slightly by 0.4%, reflecting fewer items per basket, but I wouldn’t be too worried about this since there was a stronger footfall. Additionally, DTLR's gross margin expanded 40 bps y/y to 38%, which, coupled with better cost control—enterprise-level SG&A improved 94 bps y/y as a percentage of sales—drove adj. EPS to $1.43.

Macro Backdrop

I spoke about how the macro backdrop was positive for DLTR previously, and fast forward to today, it's clear to me that the economic environment has remained in favor of DLTR. While there has been moderating inflation, the reality, as I see it, is that the impact of previous inflationary price increases has continued to exert pressure on household budgets. The tariff situation has only made things worse, as US consumers are the ones paying the tariffs. In some situations, the tariffs have made some importers stop/delay product imports, as it is no longer economical to do so. This results in undersupply of certain goods (local supply cannot meet demand), which results in price increases. The recent update by McKinsey on the US consumer base is a good read, and I strongly recommend readers to check it out.

The key chart here, I think, is the one showing that lower-income consumers are the most likely to trade down in response to tariffs. This elevated price sensitivity is an added layer of tailwind that works in favor of DLTR. And this is a reason why I think the multi-price point [MPP] strategy will continue to work very well.

In my prior update, I was positive on the multi-price point [MPP] strategy after seeing how the MPP stores outperform non-MPP stores. This 1Q25 result update, along with the macro development, instilled more confidence in me that this MPP strategy is a structural growth driver. In the quarter, MPP stores again outpaced traditional ones, with SSSG about 200 bps higher, and what is even more impressive is that new stores with MPP enabled from day one saw a 15–20% total sales uplift. This tells me that there is still latent demand from value-conscious shoppers, and it makes a lot of sense for management to double down on this front. In 1Q25 alone, DLTR converted 966 additional stores to MPP, bringing the total to >5,000, which makes it well on its way to the 7,000 store target by the end of FY25. Based on the demand strength, if DLTR can continue to execute (convert/open stores as expected), there is a clear path ahead for DLTR to drive up SSSG via increased traffic and basket growth.

Gross Margin

Given the margin headwinds seen in 4Q24 (higher shrink and distribution costs), the Dollar Tree banner’s 1Q25 gross margin performance was, in my opinion, a clear positive. Gross margin improved to 38%, up from 37.6% in 1Q24, and this is despite continued shrink headwinds and a mix-shift toward lower-margin consumables. What I took away was that DLTR has a viable way to offset these headwinds so long as it continues to grow SSSG (drives up occupancy fixed cost leverage). And if DLTR can fix its shrink issue, DLTR's gross margin profile should be structurally improved. Combating against shrink is a tough thing to do, and as much as I like to see improvement here, I think DLTR delivering gross margin expansion in 1Q25 sends a positive message to the market that gross margin can continue to expand.

That said, one area to consider here is that risk from Section 301 tariff reinstatement, which management noted could hit 2H25 gross margin by up to 120 bps. This may not sound like a big hit to gross margin, but remember DLTR is a fixed-cost business, and its adj. net margin is only ~6%. As such, the 120 bps impact to gross margin, adjusted for tax, could easily translate into a 90 bps hit to net margin (or 15% hit to earnings).

Capital Returns

One area that would make me more bullish on DLTR is the return of capital to shareholders. In 1Q25, DLTR repurchased 1.15 million shares for $149.6 million and still has a sizeable $1.05 billion remaining under its current authorization. That equates to about 0.6% per quarter, or ~2.5% annualized if the trend holds for the rest of the year, which is alright, but it is not that amazing yet.

However, with DLTR now operating under a cleaner model, its MPP strategy is working very well in lifting SSSG, and with gross margin returning to expansion, DLTR should start to see a structurally better free cash flow [FCF] profile that gives it more flexibility to buy back shares.

Valuation

Although the share price has risen by a fair bit since my last update, I don’t think the valuation is showing expensive at all. DLTR is still trading at near its -1 standard deviation over the past 10 years (16x NTM PE). And this is despite a cleaner business model, a continuous favorable macro backdrop, and a solid strategy [MPP] that provides a clear path ahead for SSSG improvement. I believe this is too cheap. Dollar General Corp. (DG), with a similar expected growth outlook (mid-single-digits), trades at a way higher multiple: it trades at 19x. I actually think DG could be worth a lot more than 19x since it has the potential to accelerate revenue growth to >10% (1Q25 Dollar Tree Banner growth was 11.3%). For reference, Five Below (FIVE) trades at 25x with a low-teens revenue growth outlook.

Risk

Shrink is still a significant issue, and if it gets worse, gross margin could erode by a lot more. The growing mix of lower-margin consumables also adds pressure to margin, especially if discretionary categories don’t rebound. Lastly, tariff-related risks are real. Any reinstatement or escalation could cut into gross margins.

Conclusion

My view for DLTR is a buy rating. The 1Q25 results reinforced my confidence in the business—SSSG is healthy, gross margins are expanding despite shrink and mix headwinds, and the MPP rollout is clearly driving incremental traffic and sales lift. With macro tailwinds still favorable, a cleaner business model post-Family Dollar, and strong execution on strategic initiatives, DLTR is well-positioned for continued growth.

data.title: Tesla: Will It Be Enough?
data.description:
Thesis Summary

The much-anticipated Model Y refresh, the Juniper, is beginning to have an impact on sales across the globe.

Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) has scored some major wins in Norway and Australia, but sales continue to struggle worldwide.

The Juniper is certainly a sophisticated piece of equipment and provides some key upgrades to the previous Model Y.

But will this be enough?

The Model Y revamp is certainly a step in the right direction, but may not be enough to actually buck the trend.

Any real discussion around Tesla as an investment must also consider the outlook for full self-drive ("FSD") and robotaxis, which we should know a lot more about by next quarter.

Mode Y: Moving The Needle

In the face of declining sales worldwide, Tesla actually scored two meaningful wins last month.

In Norway, an EV leader in Europe, Tesla’s sales jumped 213%, selling 2,346 Model Ys. Over in Australia, where the new Model Y has just launched, Tesla had its best month in almost a year.

The company delivered 3,897 vehicles, and the Model Y represented over 90% of these sales. However, it’s important to note that the total brand growth in Australia was up only 9.3%, with sales of older models like the Model 3 collapsing.

The Model Y Has Been A Hit, With Good Reason

The Juniper is a step in the right direction, and something I pointed out months ago would be a key part of the strategy to re-ignite Tesla sales.

The Juniper boasts a sleeker design that also enhances aerodynamics. The interior has improved lighting and materials, giving it superior comfort.

Most importantly, the range has been extended to 353 miles for the long-range variant and is, of course, equipped with Tesla’s latest Hardware 4 and FSD.

At a price point of $42,990 – $52,490, it is competitively priced while still offering the custom Tesla feel.

But Is This Enough?

These regional wins are the exception, not the rule.

The latest data from across Europe shows a different story. In April, Tesla ranked only 10th among EV makers, falling behind BYD Company Limited (OTCPK:BYDDY). In Europe, sales fell 49% YoY, while in China, sales fell 15% YoY.

This represents a stark contrast from the broader European EV market which is actually growing.

In the U.S. too, Tesla is losing market share, with its sales now accounting for under 50% of the total EV sales in the country.

To be fair, China's sales of Model Y and Model 3 were up 5.5% MoM.

If Tesla is going to buck the trend, we need to see a change in the MoM numbers.

In China, Tesla forecasts selling as many as 520,000 Junipers, which would be an 8.3% increase over 2024.

There are no exact figures for the U.S. and Europe. The numbers in Norway are encouraging but are not representative of what we have seen yet in the broader continent.

Tesla also launched a cheaper Model Y in the U.S. last month, which could help bolster sales moving forward.

On top of that, we should see the Tesla Model Q hit the streets later this year.

It’s Mostly About The Robotaxi Now

While I’ll certainly be keeping an eye on the latest monthly sales data, this now takes a back seat, pun intended, to the robotaxi launch.

Tesla is launching its robotaxi in Austin next week, and all eyes will be on how this develops.

The initial fleet will be made up of 10-20 driverless Model Ys, which will use Tesla’s FSD while being remotely monitored.

The objective is to take the fleet to over 1000 in a few months, and this is only a preliminary strategy until the official Cybercab begins production in 2026.

Is Tesla A Good Buy Right Now?

Tesla trades heavily on sentiment and catalysts, and there are a few on the horizon.

  • Robotaxi Launch (June 12, 2025 – Austin).

  • Tesla Energy Expansion, new Shanghai Megafactory.

  • Optimus Humanoid Robot, 5000 units by EOY.

  • Affordable Tesla (Model Q).

Furthermore, from a technical perspective, this looks like a reasonable place to buy following the big dip today.

Yes, we have broken below the 200-day EMA in what, I believe, is a wave 4 of a five-wave impulse from the lows, which isn't bullish.

However, we are exactly at the 61.8% retracement of wave 3, which is an ideal spot for a reversal.

And we are still above key support in the weekly chart.

Though today was a rough day, momentum is still on the side of the bulls, and with plenty of catalysts, I think this could still head higher.

Risks

Of course, there are plenty of things that could go wrong.

If the latest models fail to spark Tesla's sales, it will become nearly impossible to defend the legacy auto business.

With steep competition in the AV ride segment, Tesla needs to execute to perfection to keep investors interested.

And with tariffs still a threat, who knows what will happen to China and even Europe's sales, as well as any possible impact it could have on costs.

CEO Elon Musk is no longer on the good side of the White House, and that's also something to consider.

Final Thoughts

Ultimately, will all this be enough?

Cheaper models, robotaxi, Optimus, an expansion in the energy segment, and Musk’s full undivided attention.

With this current set-up and the positive momentum in the stock market, I think it’s worth a bet.

data.title: Shopping Centers Win In A Paucity Of Supply
data.description:
This article will present a simple logical chain portending a significant rise in the value of shopping centers.

  • Vacancy among existing space is minimal
  • Incremental demand remains steadily positive
  • Incremental demand will need to find new space
  • Construction cost of new retail is around $300-$500 per foot
  • Current NOI per square foot does not support $300-$500 cost to build

Therefore, NOI must rise until such a point that it supports construction. At that point, new construction will serve incremental demand, and an equilibrium will be reached.

In an effort to demonstrate soundness of the logic, let us check the truth value of each premise and show that they logically entail the conclusion.

Low Vacancy

National shopping center vacancy is at 4.1%.

That is close to a historical low and represents full occupancy because there is always going to be frictional vacancy. There is often a bit of downtime while switching tenants so 100% occupancy is not attainable. 96% is very high and essentially full.

Retail space is sufficiently scarce that tenants are actually buying up leases from bankrupt retailers so as to wiggle into full shopping centers.

Incremental demand remains high

The International Council of Shopping Centers (ICSC) conference in Vegas had an almost ebullient tone with widespread consensus among attendees that shopping centers are white hot. As an example, check out this interview from GlobeSt.

It may seem like euphoria, but the positivity was backed up by real lease signings. Multiple REITs have disclosed strong lease signings from ICSC. Small shop space is in higher demand, but even anchor space is seeing some inflows.

Since occupancy is approaching full in existing shopping centers, incremental demand beyond what is already present will need to find additional space. The challenge is that construction of new shopping centers is prohibitively expensive.

Construction costs are very high

It costs somewhere around $300-$500 per square foot to build a new shopping center. Construction costs have steadily increased across materials, labor and land.

As provided by Cushman and Wakefield

Just outfitting an existing building costs over $100-$200 per foot.

Demand in recent years has been strong enough to justify the capex to revamp old buildings. The rent upgrade REITs are getting on redevelopment of old retail space is sufficient to yield high cap rates.

However, a brand new build is far too expensive. At the 2024 REITweek I asked multiple retail REIT executives how much it costs to build a new shopping center and their answers were consistently somewhere in the $300-$600 band. Cummings group reports national average cost per foot at $394.

Note that the Cummings data screenshotted above is a couple of years old so it is likely higher today given the aforementioned increases across materials, labor and land.

NOI too low to support construction

Among the shopping center REITs we track, the average rent per square foot is $20.33.

NOI margins for shopping center REITs tend to be around 60%-75%. So NOI per foot at existing rent is somewhere in the ballpark of $14.

That is nowhere near enough to justify development costing $300-$500 per foot. Keep in mind that REITs primarily operate in the top 25% of MSAs so the cost to build specifically in REIT MSAs is likely toward the $500 side of that range.

Developments take time and involve risk. To make that worth it, one would typically demand a premium cap rate which in this case would be at least 6.5% but probably closer to 7.5%.

Assuming a 7% cap rate and a $450 cost per foot to build in REIT MSAs, development would require $31.50 per foot of NOI. At normal NOI margins that would be rent per square foot of about $45.

With average REIT shopping center rent per foot still in the low $20s it looks like construction of new shopping centers will remain close to 0 for a long time. This same cap rate check has kept construction of shopping centers at a minimal level since the Financial Crisis of 2008-2009.

Logical Conclusion

If occupancy is full and incremental demand remains high, there is not enough space to service tenant demand so the tenants will start competing for space. That demand side competition will drive rental rates up until such a point that elasticity reduces demand volume or rental rates are sufficiently high to make construction viable.

It is already happening. Shopping center REITs are routinely getting 20%+ rental rate increases on new leases. That is factually observable in the earnings reports of basically any of the shopping center REITs.

The point I am trying to illustrate in this article is that the rental rate increases have a long runway. Space will remain scarce until such a point that rental rates are high enough to justify new development. We estimate that point at rental rates of around $45 per foot.

That is approximately double rental rates on existing contracts.

There are a lot of moving parts here and one could reasonably place large error bars on our estimate. However, it is clear to industry participants that rents are far too low to justify construction. As such, new supply will remain minimal for the foreseeable future. Tight supply is a great feature for incumbent real estate owners like the shopping center REITs.

As long as demand remains moderate or better, it bodes well for rental rate growth.

Valuation

The average shopping center REIT trades at 15.9X current year estimated AFFO. Multiples of the individual companies are largely in-line with their respective leverage. Note the somewhat minimal deviation from the line in the leverage neutral valuation chart below.

We like to plot AFFO multiples on the Y axis against debt to capital on the X axis as it gives a clean perspective of the deviation in earnings multiples that do and should result from leverage.

The shopping centers seem reasonably appropriately valued relative to each other, but I think the entire sector is discounted to fair value. Given the relatively minimal disparities in valuation, I would lean toward selecting stocks based on their fundamental merits.

Brixmor (BRX) is still in the early stages of its mark-to-market on rental rates and should have a particularly long runway of growth.

Whitestone REIT (WSR) has 100% of its properties in rapid growth markets.

Consensus Net Asset Value for each company is growing nicely.

WSR and BRX trade at 67% and 83% of NAV, respectively. The average shopping center REIT trades at 82% of NAV.

I think the discounts are actually much larger than is implied by discount to NAV because the analyst NAV estimates are based on current NOI.

Analysts apply a cap rate to NOI and use that in their NAV buildout.

However, as discussed throughout this article, we believe NOI is poised to rise significantly so I posit that property value is somewhere between the NOI based NAV and replacement cost.

The average shopping center REIT in our coverage universe trades at an enterprise value per square foot of $246. Replacement cost for shopping centers in the weighted average location is somewhere around $400-$450 per foot.

Shopping center REITs are collectively trading at a massive discount to what it would cost to build their asset portfolios.

Summary

Property values and rental rates are unsustainably low in shopping centers. I posit that excluding an anomalous event, net operating income and property value will inevitably rise to the appropriate level. As such, shopping centers trading at discounted public stock valuations are opportunistic.

There is significant private investor demand for shopping centers. As building is not particularly viable, I believe that capital will be directed toward buying existing properties. This will facilitate accretive dispositions for shopping center REITs or outright buyouts of the companies.

data.title: Major Upside Risks In The Oil Market
data.description:
Short-term backwardation in the oil market has clearly steepened

Short-term backwardation in the oil market has steepened compared to the previous month and three months ago (Figure 1). Brent future spreads have widened in almost all differentials, at least until November. In fact, near-term spreads, currently at 60–70 cents ($), are at their highest level in at least a year and have rebounded strongly since early May (Figure 2). Both these indicate that the prevailing oil market is tight, meaning that supply is lower than demand.

Figure 1 – Crude Brent Futures Commodity Curve Analysis

It seems really intriguing that near term spreads are tighter despite the three successive production increases announced by selected OPEC+ members. Brent, at around $65 currently, is roughly 8% above its $60 low during May. Though it is still below the $75 level prior to the “Liberation Day” tariff proclamation on April 2nd.

Figure 2 – Brent Future Spreads (Aug 25–Sep 25)

Crude oil floating and observable land inventories are below seasonal averages

Prevailing crude oil inventories are below recent seasonal averages. Floating storage, currently estimated at approx. 75 mln bbls is almost 21% lower than the average for 2021-24. Observable land crude inventories (including the United States, ARA and China Ports) at 1.3 bln bbls presently are around 11% below the 2021-24 average (Figure 4).

Figure 3 – Floating Crude Inventories vs 2021-24 Average (mln bbls)

We believe that minimum build-up or rather inventory withdrawals is a key reason behind the steepening of the short-term backwardation in the Brent futures curve and associated tightening in near-term spreads.

Figure 4 – Observable Land Crude Inventories (US, ARA & China Ports combined) vs Seasonal Averages (mln bbls)

The actual OPEC+ supply in the market is likely to be much lower than announced

Until now, selected OPEC+ members have made declarations for three successive production increases. The first official announcement was made on April 3rd wherein eight countries led by Saudi Arabia declared to increase May 2025 production by around 411 kb/d. This was followed by two more decisions wherein output is planned to increase by an additional 411 kb/d each in June and July.

Figure 5 - OPEC+ May Production vs Targets

It is interesting to see that in May the cumulative output of these eight countries was higher by 293 kb/d compared to April. However, production was 72 kb/d below the May target (Figure 5). This is for two reasons. Firstly, Kazakhstan was already exceeding OPEC+ targets by between 300-400 kb/d and its quota has been now increased by around 15 kb/d on a monthly basis going forward. This seems to be just an attempt to gradually formalize its "over-producing". It means that with a slight increase or flat production trend, Kazakhstan will continue to exceed its revised quota. This will portray the overall production increment of the eight countries vs the target to be much smaller. Secondly, Kuwait (a key Saudi ally) produced 239 kb/d below its quota, with a flat trend in May vs April.

Figure 6 - OPEC+ June and July Production Targets

In June and July, the adjusted OPEC+ supply addition in the market is likely to be much lower than the stipulated monthly increase of 411 kb/d (Figure 6). This is because in the summer months there is a major spike in domestic fuel consumption in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait because of power generation requirements. As we show in Figures 6 and 7, summer crude demand in Saudi Arabia rises by approx. 350 kb/d and 100 kb/d respectively. Also, if Kuwait continues to produce less than its quota, then these eight countries cumulatively may end up supplying less oil in the market as opposed to an increase. This means that OPEC+ countries are unlikely to contribute to a global inventory build-up at least until August. There are reports that Saudi Arabia is pushing for additional hikes. The potential August hike is again unlikely to result in meaningful additions because of domestic consumption. Having said that, the production augmentation proclamations so far seem to be largely politically and strategically motivated, given President Trump's disliking of "high" oil prices.

Figure 6 – Saudi Arabia: Implied Crude Oil Consumption (kb/d)

Figure 7 – Kuwait: Implied Crude Oil Consumption (kb/d)

Non-OPEC+ supply growth in Q2 is likely to disappoint amidst a supportive demand picture

During Q2, IEA and US EIA are projecting growth in non-OPEC+ liquids supply to be 0.9 and 0.8 mb/d higher QoQ. A closer look at the May 2025 EIA STEO indicates that Brazil's output is projected at 4.49 mb/d in Q2, substantially higher than 3.92 mb/d in Q1. This is in sharp contrast to the detailed information in OPEC’s May monthly report pertaining to Brazil’s liquids production. As Figure 8 shows, Brazil’s production in Q2 is estimated to be lower QoQ in Q2, though overall 2024 output is likely to be higher YoY. Therefore, EIA and OPEC's outlook on the country clearly contradict.

EIA estimates Q2 crude production in the United States to be higher by 200 kb/d. The report also implies higher liquids production in the US. Nonetheless, it is clear that in the absence of production growth visible elsewhere, there are downside risks coming from non-OPEC+ liquids production. IEA and EIA are currently forecasting an oil market surplus in Q2 to be at 0.6 and 0.5 mb/d respectively. The demand picture so far is supportive. For 2025, IEA is as usual the most conservative, with demand projected to grow by 700 kb/d. OPEC is estimating an increase of 1.3 mb/d with EIA forecasting a growth of 1 mb/d. We understand that the demand situation will remain volatile given the uncertainty on the tariffs front, especially related to China.

Figure 8 – Brazil’s Quarterly Liquids Production

We feel that the market has somewhat priced-in additional supplies from Iran and Russia emanating from progress respectively on the nuclear and Russia-Ukraine peace deal. There remains considerable uncertainty on both these fronts, especially the former. Oil is still down approx. 12% YTD, with most of the negatives seem to be already built-in. We believe that in the coming weeks, WTI and Brent Crude can rebound respectively to $72 and $75 on a smaller market surplus through Q3, i.e. lower than the consensus and energy agencies. Negative production surprises on the non-OPEC+ front, smaller than declared production increments by OPEC+ and a probable US-China trade deal can be the key triggers.

data.title: Autoliv: Outperformance In The Short Term, But Don't Expect Too Much
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

In this article, I'll be looking at the Swedish company Autoliv (NYSE:ALV) - at least the company has Swedish origins, though is now an international business, selling products and services across the globe. I've been covering the business for several years at this point, and I like the focus on automotive safety and essentials. Why do I like this?

Because the company's products are appealing regardless of automotive technology. It does not matter if the cars we drive are electric, are driven by gas/petroleum, or if they're Hydrogen cars we'll be driving. It doesn't even matter all that much if they have wheels. The technologies that Autoliv is focusing on are future-proof in a way that, as long as we have vehicles that move forward, and as long as we move in them in the way we do, automotive safety in the form of airbags, seatbelts, and other things the company focuses on is completely technology-agnostic.

Despite the ties to the automotive sector, which is well-known for its cyclicality, I therefore say that this company is a quite attractive play - at least theoretically, provided that it's well-run and managed correctly.

I gave you my article update on Autoliv about 3 months back, at which point I went in. The company dropped a short time after that due to tariffs, where I loaded up more, but even given the short dip after my previous article, my stance on Autoliv has outperformed the general index, and it shows value investing to be, also in the short term, an appealing approach or way to go about things.

You can find my previous article on the company here - now I'll provide you with an update for the remainder of 2025, and see what the company could achieve here.

Autoliv - Upside intact, at least up to a certain price point

In my last article, I argued for a $110/share PT, with a fair value target up to $150/share. That's what we'll work with here - but remember that as of the time of writing this article, the price is at $101/share, so based on this, there's still upside potential in the stock (at least theoretically).

Since my last article, we have the 1Q25 results, published at about mid-April, when the previous threats of tariffs materialized and the company started dropping. The company's results at the time came in stronger than expected, with a better sales mix internationally in LVP, worse on a regional basis.

ALV saw margin expansion, though not because of sales, but internal efficiencies and improvements driven by the plan the company is going through. To that end, ALV also reduced the workforce by 1,500 individuals, with significant direct headcount reductions for the past 12 months of no less than 3,700 in the face of an organic sales growth.

The company recorded record earnings for a first quarter, with significant net profit improvement on the bottom line. It continued buying back shares, and the dividend can certainly be considered safe here.

Autoliv continues to do what I consider to be significant product innovation.

It's this technology leadership coupled with superb financial execution that we'll see above that I believe makes Autoliv attractive despite the indisputable cyclicality the company operates under due to its close ties to LVP. The LVP market is an easy one to be in, and also has been more cyclical than on a historical basis as of late, meaning LVP hasn't been growing as much as expected. Regional mix drove the company's numbers down, and the entire market for light vehicle production is a bit in a lurch at this time.

It's important not to overstate the influence of China here. Sales for the company are still 30/33% to Europe and the Americas, with APAC altogether at 37%, but China only at 17%. Another way to put it of course, is that ALV still has significant growth potential in China and Asia. The recent trends in EV sales, in Europe also, have seen an increasing favoritism for Asian and specifically Chinese EVs. BYD is seeing significant growth, for example, while native brands, like Volvo in Sweden, are simply not selling as well. We know, for instance, that BYD is now outselling Tesla in Europe, and this is despite significantly higher tariffs than Tesla is facing.

I would also characterize BYD as still being in its infancy In most European nations, so we'll see what the future for these markets hold - but according to some sources, the company's European sales have tripled/quadrupled in 1Q25 alone.

Autoliv, meanwhile, is enjoying upside from every part of the industry, due to its exposure not only to BYD models, but European models on the entire market. Almost every car model being released on the continent has some sort of Autoliv technology inside it, be it airbags, seatbelts or a combination of all of the company's technologies combined.

The company's sales mix, not on a regional level, but on a brand level, is positive. No brand represents an overconcentration of revenue risk. This even goes for single markets, like the Americas, where no car brand is even 20% of the market. The biggest are Honda, Stellantis, and Ford, but even these three giants together don't represent 50% of the company's sales in the American market.

In terms of tariff impact for NA, the company's manufacturing footprint is already somewhat simplified. Perhaps the challenge that the company has is that it has optimized manufacturing for NA, not for the USA. This means that it has assets in Mexico and Canada both, and the trends on the continent rely on a profitable interplay/trade between these nations and assets. The most significant headcount for the company is in Mexico, which has more than 3x the employees found in the USA.

It's therefore fair to say that while the company doesn't suffer from tariff impacts between the US/EU or the US/Asia, the company will see tariff impacts from tariffs levied against Canada or against Mexico. I would say these risks are lower than against Asia or even Europe, but they are certainly still there - hence also the significant short-term drop in share price we saw in conjunction with 1Q25.

These also constitute the primary risks for the investment. Aside from the obvious LVP risk, I would now say that Tariffs and import duties are the primary issue for the company. ALV products are mostly USMCA compliant, but not all, due to insufficient local content or customer-directed parts, which could make tariffs a thing. The Chinese tariffs are unlikely to have an impact on the company, but general tariffs could be different. It's the reason ALV is already negotiating with OEM's to try and adjust its footprint and sourcing to make it tariff compliant. Aside from the tariff risks and general cyclical LVP risks, I don't see many risks worth mentioning. That being said, those two risks are enough to put Autoliv in a position where I see growth as very difficult.

The current estimates are for a recovery/growth of 15-17% in 2026-2027 for EPS, based on both efficiencies and LVP growth and recovery. I would halve this estimate to be safe. The company's positive/appeal remains, arguments in the form of resilient fundamentals and market leadership coupled with a slight growth over time are enough to keep me interested here - but don't expect this company to grow quickly over a short time, because I don't believe the underlying fundamentals reflect this.

Valuation trends for the company are now as follows.

Autoliv Valuation - Upside for the long term, with added safety from fundamentals

Given the improved share price, you cannot expect as good an upside as when I last covered the company at below $90/share. We now have a price of $101, which, to my PT of $101 and my fair value estimate of $150/share, gives us about 16-17% per year, compared to over 20% per year in my last article. I don't forecast Autoliv at 15-16x P/E despite both the historical trends and the estimated earnings growth - I work with a 12.5x 2027E P/E, coming to that $150/share PT based on current estimates, and this gives us the following rate of return.

Another way you could view it is that Autoliv is worth 15x P/E, but where you'd cut the EPS growth estimates for 2026-2027 in half to be conservative - and with those estimates, the FV estimate would be around $155/share at 15x forward P/E - so about the same. You can either discount P/E or you can discount estimates - it comes to about the same, a discount.

Why do we discount?

First, because of a failure of Autoliv to consistently hit targets. Those estimates may look good, but they have little grounding in reality. On a 1-year basis, forecast accuracy for Autoliv is 0% with a 10% margin of error. The company either beats or misses negatively those estimates 40-50% of the time. This is the first reason I would be careful about any forecast estimates for Autoliv.

The second reason for discounting the company is related to growth potential. I believe that automotive/LVP growth will be slower going forward than it has been historically. Slower growth means that even in the face of increased buybacks and internal efficiencies, I would keep this company at an overall lower share price target. That's not even mentioning the competitive nature of the segment, because I believe this to be less relevant than other analysts seem to, given Autoliv's technology leadership.

However, competition comes in the form of companies BorgWarner (BWA), Magna (MGA), and Takata, primarily. I've said it before when reviewing Magna and Lear (LEA), that the entire automotive industry, including suppliers, is currently trading very cheaply. It's all about finding what I view as the "best deals" here. I believe Autoliv represents one of those best deals, given its aforementioned technology leadership and focus, unlike Borgwarner and other peers, on products that are technology-agnostic.

This does not make the peers unattractive, and indeed, I own stakes in some of them, but it does mean that I view Autoliv as the clearly more attractive of the group, which is why I stake my position in the company at such a high level.

In total, I believe Autoliv is now in a less appealing position than a few months back - but still attractive enough to consider an investment. My stance is that the company offers a more than 15% annualized upside based on what I view as a conservative NTM P/E of 14-16x for 2025-2026, and 12.5x P/E for 2027 on current estimates. The company's yield is not fantastic, but the combination of yield, safety, and upside is good enough to justify it still.

My thesis is as follows.

Thesis

  • Autoliv is one of the more solid automotive safety companies in the world, but unfortunately still has massive ties to light automotive vehicle production. The company's upside as of 1Q25 is still intact.
  • The aforementioned upside is still volatile, but the company has some excellent growth potential from a mix of improving LVP trends, better synergies, operational improvements, and other bullish potentials.
  • I now have a longer-term stake in Autoliv, with a projected timeframe of at the very least 2027. I view the primary risks as being tariffs and LVP overall, but I believe the company is positioned well enough to manage both of them quite nicely.

My short-term investment case (6-12 months) is as follows:

The narrative for the short term isn't that different from the long term. The company is attractive here, I don't see any significant catalysts outside of the standard automotive narrative that could influence the company here, and any sort of negative influence is likely to be short-lived. Tariffs are the big "if", but if the last month has been any indication, this will continue to fluctuate.

My medium-to-long-term investment case (up to and beyond 5 years) is as follows:

For the longer term, I would argue that there are few downsides to the company until 2027E as an investment - which is also the reason why I am positive about the stock here. I argue that any downside catalyst is insignificant in the face of LVP upside and cost/savings upside, which I view as likely in the next 2-3 years.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of €140/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a “Buy rating as of June 2025.

data.title: Understanding Palantir With A Little Help From Ben Graham (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
For The Record

I believe it's worth mentioning, as a prologue to my central message to you today, that I have been a huge believer in Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR) since its direct listing.

The stock is up nearly 800% in my LASR Index, and it is, to be sure, still a 4% position, even after the huge selling I did earlier this year.

I believe in the business, its mission, and its leadership, and, today, I am in no sense disputing that Palantir is a brilliant business that's growing rapidly while generating huge free cash flow.

In my view, Palantir is one of the best, most important businesses on earth. Palantir stock and its valuation aside, I could not be more bullish on the company that is Palantir.

With that in mind, let's begin exploring Palantir's valuation today.

Same Stock Market, Different Year

Throughout the history of the modern stock market, dating back to Jonathan's Coffee House in the 17th century, financial bubbles have formed and subsequently deflated, resulting in significant financial loss.

It's logical to think that, following the disasters of the original financial bubbles, such as the South Sea Bubble, investors of those early eras would have learned lessons that they then passed on to the next generation of investors.

Equipped with the lessons of the past, most would likely expect that subsequent generations of investors would avoid investing in and inadvertently creating themselves financial bubbles.

This is, unfortunately, not how the market works in practice, and, despite countless financial bubbles forming over the last 350 years or so of stock market history, new financial bubbles form almost yearly.

To be sure, previous generations of investors have actually gone to extraordinary lengths to teach subsequent generations about the dangers of financial bubbles. These teachings have been codified in some of the most important financial texts in history, such as Ben Graham's Security Analysis.

Below, I shared an excerpt from Ben Graham's Security Analysis, in which he articulated the prevailing view of stock valuations in the 1920s, and thereby attempted to teach subsequent generations of investors about the dangers of investing in overvalued stocks, e.g., stocks that could be characterized as having become financial bubbles.

Stocks Regarded As Attractive Irrespective Of Their Prices

In 1934, Ben Graham published Security Analysis following the Great Depression and the associated stock market collapse.

What's so interesting to me is that the language he cited as being dangerous 92 years ago is the same language that often permeates the consciousness of market participants today.

In fact, in the midst of modern financial bubbles, we often read this language exactly, i.e., investors say we've entered a "new-era" for valuations or "just buy the leaders" and don't worry about valuation.

While there is certainly value in "buying the leader" within a given industry, taken to an extreme, it's invariably a strategy destined to fail.

Today, I read a comment in the comments section of the latest Palantir news in which the author of the comment shared that we should get rid of the price to sales valuation metric as it is no longer useful. The commenter shared that traditional valuation philosophies should be discarded, as they're not valuable in understanding Palantir.

Of course, because we are students of stock market history, we know that this is a misguided way of thinking, and, in fact, has been used as a justification for financial bubbles and their associated speculative behavior since the dawn of the stock market.

There have been many financial bubbles throughout the centuries, and, almost certainly, the same language that Ben Graham cited and which I witnessed today in relation to Palantir was uttered.

For instance, there was Railway Mania in the mid-19th century, at the dawn of the development of the railway transportation network in the U.S. and the Dot Com Bubble, at the dawn of the modern Internet.

RailWay Mania Inflates & Subsequently Deflates

Dot Com Bubble Inflates & Deflates

It's worth noting that financial bubbles arise fairly frequently.

We've discussed three prominent examples, e.g., the conclusion of the Roaring Twenties, Railway Mania, and the Dot Com Bubble; however, financial bubbles are actually a pretty routine occurrence in the stock market.

For instance, in the last 20 years, there have been two fairly noteworthy green energy bubbles: One in the 2000s and one in the early 2020s.

The First Alternative Energy Bubble (Renewable Energy Industrial Index: RENIXX)

There have been two noteworthy 3D printing bubbles: one in the 2010s and one in the 2020s.

3D Printing Bubble Of The 2010s & Early 2020s

There has been at least one cannabis bubble in the last decade.

And, of course, there was the software bubble of the early 2020s, which we'll lean on to understand Palantir's current valuation in just a moment.

Today, we're almost certainly in the midst of a quantum computing bubble. It's actually been pretty wild to witness the degree of speculation there. There are presently companies trading for billions of dollars, with, in one case, a five figure revenue tally for 2025.

In short, financial bubbles do arise, and, interestingly, their characteristics are almost always identical to past bubbles, e.g., market participants begin saying things like, "Past valuations don't apply in today's market. We're in a new-era."

The notion that the desirability of a common stock was entirely independent of its price seems incredibly absurd. Yet the new-era theory led directly to this thesis.

Ben Graham, Security Analysis.

I mean I read nearly this exact language today in relation to Palantir... 92 years after this language was used to justify the excesses of the Roaring Twenties.

To close this section, valuations matter. They are as real and tangible as the laws of physics themselves. For the sake of brevity, I won't delve into the specific mechanics underlying the value of a stock, i.e., the four points of stock value; however, it's worth noting these ideas for our situational awareness.

We Already Know How This Ends

Understanding what will come of Palantir's stock in the next five years is fairly easy.

It's easy because the aftermath of today's Palantir exuberance has been playing out for the last five years via the stock of Palantir's closest peer, Snowflake (SNOW).

With five years of data following Snowflake's exuberant bout of pricing in 2020 and 2021, we can reasonably assume what will transpire for Palantir in the years ahead.

In 2020, Snowflake's narrative and valuation were virtually identical to Palantir's narrative and valuation today.

Snowflake was The Data Super Cloud.

Warren Buffett bought in. Frank Slootman, the man who built one of the greatest software businesses of all time, ServiceNow (NOW), was taking Snowflake to the next level.

It was the data stock to own.

Using this logic, investors bid Snowflake's stock to incredible heights, bringing its valuation to 150-200x sales in late 2020.

Snowflake's EV/Sales And Revenue Growth Rate

In the eyes of the market, Snowflake was the big winner of the data cloud market. As I mentioned, it was the Data Super Cloud, according to the software investing community at the time.

Palantir, as an aside, was the weaker, less well-executed data business with a sketchy management team, so the narrative went. As we know, these two narratives have very ironically flipped, and the extremes have now gone in the complete opposite direction.

From the point in late 2020 in which Snowflake was seen as the Data Super Cloud to today, Snowflake's stock price has fallen 50% or so, down from $300/share to $200/share.

The Data Super Cloud, so it was called in 2020 and 2021, has generated -50% returns over five years.

The casual observer might think, based on this stock price performance: "Oh, well growth must have been poor. The business probably didn't do as well as market participants expected. The thesis didn't work out."

And that casual observer would be wrong.

In fact, Snowflake hasn't just performed well from late 2020. It's performed unbelievably well.

Its performance has been simply breathtaking. Off the charts great.

In 2020, Snowflake grew 123%.

In 2021, it grew 106%.

In 2022, it grew 69%: From over ~$1.2B in sales to ~$2.1B in sales. Incredible growth at huge scale.

In 2023, it grew 36%.

In 2024 and currently, it's sustained ~30% growth (no deceleration in Q1'25 (CY)) at giant scale.

Snowflake has been one of the best businesses in the entire stock market for the last five years.

Period.

It's grown its sales roughly 10x in just about five years.

Snowflake Roughly 10x'd Its Sales From 2020 To 2025

Staggeringly great performance.

This is growth that software investors may not witness for decades to come for a public software business.

I mean I cannot overstate how phenomenal this level of growth has been and how phenomenal Snowflake has performed as a business.

And its stock has declined 50%!

900-1,000% profitable revenue growth in five years.

...

50% stock price decline.

So we're left with the question: How could a business that just grew high margin, free cash flow generative sales at an almost unbelievable rate for five straight years generate -50% returns in that same period?

In short, valuations matter a lot.

There is no new-era. There never will be a new-era. There is a rigidly defined valuation standard that is quite literally coded into the laws of physics.

Turning to Palantir, which is now seen as a transcendental entity for whom valuation does not matter (so the narrative has gone recently).

Today, it trades at well over 100x sales on a fully diluted basis, while growing at ~40%.

Palantir's EV/Sales & Quarterly Revenue Growth Rate

Palantir diluted shareholders by ~6% in 2024, and, should it dilute by 5% in 2025, it will have about 2.7B shares outstanding by the end of 2025. 2.7B times $130/share = $350B in market cap today.

On a trailing twelve months basis, Palantir has generated about $3.3B in sales.

So we're well over 100x sales alongside ~40% growth.
